name: assume
description: If you assume a proposition, you can deduce it — because you just said it's true.

name: ex-falso
description: From a contradiction ($\bot$), you can derive any formula $p$. The principle of explosion — *ex falso quodlibet*.

name: fix
description: Introduce a fresh term variable into the context — like assume, but for terms.

name: subst
description: Uniformly replace atoms with formulas in a proven judgement. The proof structure is independent of the particular symbols — a structural rule.
example:
1. ⊢ P → P
2. (subst 1 (P (and A B)))  →  ⊢ (A ∧ B) → (A ∧ B)

name: not-elim
description: From $\lnot p$ derive $p \to \bot$ — negation unwraps into an implication to falsity.

name: not-intro
description: If assuming $p$ leads to both $q$ and $\lnot q$ (a contradiction), then $\lnot p$ must hold. *Reductio ad absurdum* — reduce to the absurd.

name: dne
description: From $\lnot \lnot p$ derive $p$. Eliminate a double negation — classical logic's escape hatch.

name: ->-elim
description: If $p$ implies $q$, and you have $p$, then you have $q$. The classic syllogism — *modus ponens*.
example:
1. (assume P)  →  {P} ⊢ P
2. P → Q provided as premise
3. (->-elim 2 1) → Q

name: ->-intro
description: If from assuming $p$ you can reach $q$, then $p$ implies $q$ — and you can safely drop $p$ from the context.

name: forall-elim
description: From $\forall x.\; \varphi$, derive $\varphi$ with any term $t$ substituted for $x$. What holds for everything holds for any particular thing.

name: forall-intro
description: If you prove $\varphi$ for an arbitrary $x$ not appearing elsewhere, the proof holds for all $x$. Universal generalisation.
example:
1. (fix x)  →  {x} ⊢ x
2. (assume (App P x))  →  {x, P(x)} ⊢ P(x)
3. (->-intro (App P x) 2)  →  {x} ⊢ P(x) → P(x)
4. (forall-intro x 3)  →  ⊢ ∀x. P(x) → P(x)

name: exists-elim
description: From an existential claim, open a witness $y$, prove the conclusion $\psi$ under the assumption $\varphi[y/x]$, and discharge — provided $y$ doesn't escape into $\Delta$ or $\psi$.

name: exists-intro
description: If you have a concrete example $\varphi$ involving term $t$, generalise it to $\exists x.\; \varphi$ by replacing $t$ with a fresh variable $x$. From the particular to the existential.

name: and-elim-l
description: If you know $p \land q$, then you know $p$ — the left half of the conjunction.

name: and-elim-r
description: If you know $p \land q$, then you know $q$ — the right half of the conjunction.

name: and-intro
description: If you've proved $p$ and you've proved $q$, then you've proved $p \land q$ — they're both true together.

name: or-elim
description: If $p \lor q$ is true, and both $p$ and $q$ independently lead to $r$, then $r$ is true regardless. Reason by cases — exhaust the possibilities.
rule: \frac{\Gamma \vdash p \lor q \quad \Delta_1, p \vdash r \quad \Delta_2, q \vdash r}{\Gamma \cup \Delta_1 \cup \Delta_2 \vdash r}

name: or-intro-l
description: If you've proved $p$, you've proved $p \lor q$ — because the left side is already true. The $q$ can be anything.

name: or-intro-r
description: If you've proved $q$, you've proved $p \lor q$ — because the right side is already true. The $p$ can be anything.

name: box-intro
description: Necessitation (NEC): from ∅ ⊢ A derive ∅ ⊢ □A. The context must be empty — necessity only applies to theorems.

name: box-elim
description: K axiom: from □(A → B) and □A derive □B. Modus ponens under the box.

name: diamond-def
description: ◇-definition: unfold ◇A to ¬□¬A.

name: diamond-def-rev
description: ◇-definition reverse: fold ¬□¬A back to ◇A.

name: top-intro
description: From anything, truth follows. ⊤ is always provable — an axiom requiring no premises.
